entry.title: Pure
entry.content:
Lately, intermittently, I’ve come across a classic interview by Wang Lifen about Jet Li. When I watched the complete video again, I found myself reflecting on a multitude of topics—thinking, the One Foundation, the Internet, humanity, faith, and so much more. This led to a new understanding and sentiment.

Jet Li understood the concept of Yin and Yang when he was only 11 years old, grasping the essence of Tai Chi. It wasn’t until he was around 30 that he began to glimpse the truth: the world isn’t just black and white.

Speaking of science, Jet Li shared:

"The mysteries of the universe that humanity has mastered over five thousand years are truly limited. The scientific knowledge we possess is quite confined; it's called science precisely because it isn't scientific."

I never expected to hear this from Emperor of Kung Fu, but it's truly remarkable that someone who didn't graduate from elementary school, who has remained humble about his lack of education, could arrive at such a profound realization.

Regarding science, many people nowadays lack an understanding, thinking that science equates to truth. In fact, it’s not. The correctness of science is temporary, and it’s through constant negation and revision that we arrive at truth. There might not even be any absolute truth in this world. The only truth might be the continuous process of seeking the truth.

Due to martial arts and the film “Shaolin Temple,” Jet Li gained insight into the world, comprehending movies and the universe. As a teenager, he developed an entirely new perception of the world, which he continually pondered. He questioned why people always said, “Martial artists are simple-minded with well-developed limbs.” He spent half his life seeking evidence and disproving this notion, dedicating himself to learning to cultivate as much wisdom as possible, which he indeed achieved.

This realization motivated Jet Li to leave the system, even if it meant breaking his leg. His determination led to a serendipitous leg injury, and he found himself ecstatic about it, sensing that fate was on his side. Perhaps it was youthful ignorance or youthful aspiration that led him to such an outlook. He ventured out, giving birth to numerous exceptional films, but films were merely a fraction of his excellence.

He is someone who contemplates deeply: he reflects on whether something is problematic when everyone does it. He ponders how to be more efficient. These two ways of thinking became deeply ingrained in him at a young age, perhaps influenced by his early encounter with the concept of yin and yang. It’s remarkable; although exposed to this concept early, not everyone can grasp its essence.

Fortunately, after his introduction to Zen Buddhism, the constant yearning led him to the edge of 「Dao」, a stroke of immense luck.

He continuously reads books, biographies of famous people, the life stories of Marie Curie, Stalin, Hitler, Edison, and selections from Deng Xiaoping, among others. He reads classics, including the Bible and the Quran.

When he was young, Jet Li had a dream—to water flowers. However, when he shared this dream with those around him, they labeled him as lacking ambition, direction, and courage, accusing him of being a coward who didn’t aspire for greatness.

The Little Prince once had such a pure dream, unable to comprehend why so many people were so complicated, so impure.

And now, what I’m tirelessly pursuing is nothing more than a pure dream, akin to watering flowers—perhaps one or several dreams.

entry.title: Simple
entry.content:
Many years ago, I might have been someone who delved into various fields of knowledge, and perhaps I still am to this day, but now I’m captivated by a very simple principle: “Keep it simple.”

This principle, originally known as “K.I.S.S” - Keep it simple, stupid.

Albert Einstein once said:

“Everything should be made as simple as possible, but no simpler.”

“It can scarcely be denied that the supreme goal of all theory is to make the irreducible basic elements as simple and as few as possible without having to surrender the adequate representation of a single datum of experience.”

For many things, we often think they are too complex, too complex to understand. In fact, the world can be broken down into simplicity. Zhang Chaoyang once shared this insight.

I recall Fu Peirong discussing Western philosophy in class, mentioning one of Heraclitus’ thoughts: “All things come from fire.”

This perspective may seem erroneous now, but at that time, I found it impressive. He was seeking the most fundamental construction of the world and drew a conclusion.

Perhaps this is one of the origins of materialism, and it’s quite significant.

When discussing logic, we also need to clarify the concept first, as the concept is the most basic unit of semantic understanding.

Back during my student days, when I first encountered chemical molecules and atoms, one day our chemistry teacher told us that both diamond and charcoal are composed of carbon elements. However, diamond is C60, and charcoal is C4, just differing in structural bonds.

In the workplace, we often break down tasks in planning, aiming to simplify tasks to the utmost, to the point where they are crystal clear.

The periodic table of elements is a marvelous thing, and it remains so to this day. Bill Gates has an element wall in his office, reportedly collecting physical samples of all elements except radioactive ones.

I’ve also attempted to understand the simplicity of this world, trying to peel away the layers of its various exteriors.

The world originally might have consisted of individual entities composed of these elements, and then due to various connections, the world gained its rich diversity, forming its shape.

More than 90% of things in this world can be broken down to a simple level of comprehension. If they cannot, it only suggests that we are still unable to understand, unable to delve into the truth and essence, requiring continual exploration.

The world of computers has only 0 and 1. Is it possible that our world is the same?

entry.title: Hello World
entry.content:
Welcome to Hexo! This is your very first post. Check documentation for more info. If you get any problems when using Hexo, you can find the answer in troubleshooting or you can ask me on GitHub.
